Which traits & leagues predict Bundesliga success?

After we shortlist portable traits (stability → redundancy), Phase 2 tests what actually lines up with Year-1 Bundesliga success. Then dive into player examples with before/after radars.

How we define Bundesliga success (Phase 2)

Our primary success marker is minutes played in the player’s first Bundesliga season (Year-1 minutes). We treat more Y1 minutes as playing-time success — the club gave the inbound player a real role. Trait tests, league comparisons, and regression Δ Y1 min all use this outcome unless noted otherwise.

We also show Y1 minutes percentile (rank in cohort) and stable-trait score (profile fit on shortlisted traits). Transfermarkt market-value change would be the top outcome in a pro model, but we do not scrape Transfermarkt (ToS); minutes are our open-data proxy.

Method

  • Primary success marker: Y1 minutes — first-season Bundesliga minutes played
  • Secondary outcomes: Y1 minutes percentile · stable-trait score
  • Trait test: Spearman ρ — prior-season trait percentile vs each outcome
  • League test: mean Y1 outcomes by feeder league (FBref, N=329)
  • League × trait: within each trait, which feeder league’s prior profile best predicts Y1 minutes?
  • Regression (done · exploratory): OLS on Y1 minutes — league dummies vs Serie A + prior minutes + traits (Big-5 subset N=117)

Phase 2 includes exploratory OLS (not a validated forecast).

Overall feeder leagues (FBref)

Descriptive means by feeder (n≥8). Success tests use final shortlists (FBref N=329 · 32 traits).

Traits most indicative of BL success (overall)

FBref (N=329)

Higher prior percentile on defensive box work, final-third passing, blocks & aerials ↔ more Y1 minutes.

StatsBomb (N=96)

Directional only — many priors are World Cup/Euro minutes, not domestic league seasons.

Which league is most predictive per trait?

For each shortlisted trait, best feeder by Spearman ρ (prior %ile → Y1 minutes). Split at p < 0.05.

Stronger signal (p < 0.05)

Best-league cells that clear the usual significance bar — still associative, small within-league n.

CategoryTraitBest feederρpn

Noisier / weaker (p ≥ 0.05)

Same ranking method, but the “best” league is still a weak or underpowered cell — do not over-read these.

CategoryTraitBest feederρpn
Reading ρ: Each row is the relatively best feeder for that trait, not a forecast. Top table = clearer associations (p<0.05). Bottom = highest ρ among noisy cells — “least weak,” not strong evidence.

Regression — exploratory OLS (Big-5 subset)

Descriptive means aren’t enough. OLS estimates how much each Big-5 league adds on Y1 minutes vs Serie A, then asks whether prior trait percentiles explain the gap.

League effects (M1 · vs Serie A)

LeagueΔ Y1 minp

Prior traits (M3 · controlling for league)

Trait pctΔ Y1 minp

Stat dictionary (glossary)

Definitions for every shortlisted trait in the player explorer, plus outcome terms used on this page.

Outcomes & methods

How to read percentiles

PRE and Y1 cells are percentiles 0–100 vs Bundesliga position peers in the cohort. 50 = average, 90 ≈ top decile. Stability r (on explorer metrics) is prior→Y1 rate correlation from Step 1 — separate from success-indicator ρ.

Shortlisted traits

Bundesliga Transfer Traits · Cal Berkeley · results/SUCCESS_INDICATORS.md · README.md (feeder regression tables)

Player explorer — before & after Bundesliga

Radar slices = style categories. Each slice averages shortlisted metrics in that group. Right panel lists metrics under each slice. Toggle StatsBomb (N=96 · 7 traits) ↔ FBref (N=329 · 32 traits).

Stable-trait score = average of BL Y1 percentiles on listed traits (vs position peers).

What the numbers mean (percentile scale)

Every PRE / Y1 cell is a percentile 0–100 vs Bundesliga position peers.
AVG = 50 · TOP = 90 · Min ≈ 0 · Max ≈ 100.
Category radar · blue = BL Y1 · red dotted = prior
